questions.specification: A global electronics company is redesigning its product packaging and logistics system to reduce environmental impact across its product life cycle. Designers are using LCA to make data-informed decisions.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a delivery truck being loaded with cardboard shipping boxes.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/nrooIInfvN2kLJvuqgBNo)

Identify the life-cycle stage represented in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Distribution and transport <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two reasons why transport contributes significantly to a product's environmental footprint.
parts.markscheme:
- Fuel consumption leads to CO₂ and NOx emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Packaging for protection increases material usage <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows people throwing old electronic items into a household waste bin.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/yz2zV-mjXQY_JpAu6VIWE)

Describe three environmental consequences of disposing of electronic products in general waste.
parts.markscheme:
- Releases toxic substances like lead and mercury into soil and groundwater <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Misses opportunity to recover valuable materials like copper or rare earths <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adds to landfill mass, increasing methane emissions and space usage <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare landfill disposal with e-waste recycling for electronic appliances.
parts.markscheme:
- Landfill disposal leads to long-term pollution and resource loss <marks>1 mark</marks>
- E-waste recycling reduces pollution by safely extracting harmful substances <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycling recovers reusable materials, lowering demand for virgin mining <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycling requires infrastructure and energy, but results in lower overall footprint <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a bar chart comparing CO2 for plastic, glass, and aluminum packaging from raw material extraction to disposal.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d277726e-e054-4c17-ad48-7f3a21ee136f)

Explain two ways designers could use the data in **Figure 3** to make more sustainable decisions.
parts.markscheme:
- Choose materials with lower overall CO₂ emissions (lower carbon footprint) across the life cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify stages (e.g. extraction vs recycling) with highest CO₂ emissions to target redesign or process improvements <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the usefulness and limitations of life-cycle analysis when comparing different packaging materials.
parts.markscheme:
- Useful for assessing total energy use, emissions, and material impacts across stages <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides quantitative data for transparent and informed decision-making <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables cradle-to-grave assessment rather than focusing only on manufacture or disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May not capture local variations in recycling systems or user behavior <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complex data collection and assumptions can affect accuracy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Results might not account for indirect or social impacts (e.g. labor practices) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A product development team is analysing the environmental footprint of food containers to improve packaging choices for an upcoming eco-conscious brand launch. Their focus is on raw material sourcing, energy use, and end-of-life impact.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a stack of raw aluminium sheets ready for industrial use.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ibl9XOt8W_QdrJ5ilcfXi)

Identify the life-cycle stage shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Raw material extraction and processing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two environmental impacts of aluminium extraction.
parts.markscheme:
- Requires large amounts of electricity, especially in electrolysis <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Causes land degradation and water pollution from bauxite mining <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a compostable paper-based food container next to a traditional plastic container.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Amf5d913u5njPY8UlxgJM)

Describe three factors a designer would consider when selecting between paper and plastic containers in terms of environmental impact.
parts.markscheme:
- Biodegradability and landfill persistence <marks>1 mark</marks>
 
- Energy and emissions from material processing <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Recyclability or compostability infrastructure availability <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the end-of-life (disposal) stage for paper and plastic containers.
parts.markscheme:
- Paper containers decompose more easily, especially in composting systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
 
- Plastic containers persist in landfills and oceans for decades <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Paper recycling is more energy-efficient than plastic recycling <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Plastic contamination can lower recycling rates or clog machinery <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a warehouse storing large quantities of packaged food containers before being shipped to retailers.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/63746e4f-5fce-4e12-b9e2-3965364f57a2)

Explain how storage and warehousing affect the life-cycle environmental footprint of a product.
parts.markscheme:
- Requires energy for lighting, heating/cooling <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Occupies physical space and contributes to embodied energy from infrastructure <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Inventory mismanagement can lead to expired or wasted goods <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Transport to and from warehouses adds carbon emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how life-cycle analysis helps a designer choose between two packaging options based on sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
- LCA provides a comprehensive view across all stages: extraction, manufacture, use, and disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies hidden energy or pollution costs in processes like shipping or disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Enables designers to make informed trade-offs between durability, cost, and environmental impact <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Helps meet regulatory and consumer sustainability expectations <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Supports circular design thinking through recycling or reuse strategies <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Encourages innovation in materials and processes for long-term sustainability <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
What does this diagram of a product’s life from extraction to disposal represent in sustainability analysis?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXexYClf7AR7prECQT4pJRD0iV39xlQixlqSsO0Pv_c_rLQXR949rtF2e2qL-35ArJElHSm_ZdXfgj_RHdP2EF-v031s9NU9-cgZ6a3S6eTKbVrCdR31lOSP6F25RLELnVw9wnPkGA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: A cost-benefit comparison of production speed
The market segmentation process in advertising
A life-cycle analysis of environmental impacts
An engineering tolerance study for manufacturing

questions.specification:
Which life-cycle stage does this image of a factory represent?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/baacf275-a45b-41f9-aae5-93d4313b5952)
Which life-cycle phase has the highest energy input for small personal electronics, such as a smartphone?

options.markscheme: According to the IB Design Technology curriculum, the pre-production phase of small personal electronics (which includes the energy-intensive extraction and refining of rare materials) typically represents the highest energy input for this product category.

Production involves the manufacturing and assembly of the device. While energy-intensive, it is generally less energy-consuming than the extraction and processing of materials (pre-production) for modern miniaturized electronics.

The use phase is the highest energy consumer for large household appliances (white goods), but for energy-efficient personal electronics like smartphones, it is significantly lower than the production-related phases.
The disposal phase typically accounts for the smallest portion of total life-cycle energy input.

parts.content: Explain why ecotoxicity and resource depletion are key concerns in life-cycle analysis.
parts.markscheme:
- State that ecotoxicity affects biodiversity and aquatic ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that resource depletion threatens long-term material availability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that these impacts influence design choices for sustainability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how LCA helps minimize these effects by informing material selection <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each justified concern, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification:
## Question
Which of the following is a valid reason to conduct a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) during the early design stage?

options.content: To forecast and reduce environmental impacts

questions.specification: A manufacturer wants to reduce $\text{CO}_2$ emissions. Which stage(s) of the product life cycle should they optimize?

options.markscheme: Optimizing only the use stage is insufficient as it ignores potential emissions in other parts of the life cycle.
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) requires a holistic approach, considering every stage from pre-production to disposal to ensure a total reduction in $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.
Optimizing only the disposal stage does not address the significant emissions typically generated during manufacturing and distribution.
Transportation is only one component; a comprehensive reduction strategy must look at the entire product life cycle.

parts.content: Analyse how Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) can influence material selection in product design.
parts.markscheme:
- Analyse the use of non-renewable resources; LCA highlights their depletion and environmental cost, prompting designers to select more sustainable alternatives. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaluate the embodied energy of materials; LCA allows designers to prioritise materials with lower energy requirements during extraction and processing to reduce the carbon footprint. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prioritise the end-of-life stage; LCA encourages the selection of biodegradable or recyclable materials to minimise landfill waste and support a circular economy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify toxicity and pollution risks; LCA tracks hazardous emissions across all stages, leading designers to avoid materials that pose risks to human health or ecosystems. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examine the potential for material reuse; LCA data helps in choosing durable materials that maintain integrity for refurbishment or repurposing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Assess the balance between material durability and life-span; LCA ensures materials are selected to optimise product longevity and reduce overall resource consumption. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each well-reasoned influence, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following is a drawback of performing a full life cycle analysis (LCA)?

options.content: It eliminates the need for prototyping
It fails to account for carbon emissions
It provides no insight into material sourcing

questions.specification: Why is the recycling stage important in the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of an aluminium can?

options.content: It adds branding value for marketing purposes
options.markscheme: Marketing and branding are not primary objectives of the recycling stage within a Life Cycle Assessment.

options.content: It reduces energy use and resource depletion
options.markscheme: Recycling aluminium saves significant amounts of energy (up to 95%) compared to primary production from bauxite ore, thereby reducing both energy consumption and resource depletion.

options.content: It maximizes consumer appeal through design
options.markscheme: Consumer appeal is typically addressed during the design and development stages of the product life cycle.
Mass production capabilities are determined by manufacturing processes and facilities rather than the recycling stage.

questions.specification: What feature distinguishes full LCAs from other environmental tools?

options.content: Focus on a single stage of a product’s life cycle (for example, manufacturing only)
Evaluation based mainly on cost–benefit analysis rather than environmental impacts across the whole life cycle

questions.specification: During which life-cycle stage is the environmental impact of raw material extraction assessed?

What does this chart showing different materials' carbon footprints help identify?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdtbLtM7ieSzfIM4smHvQqfpakPx604MWGXNsNLd-Sns1P5VwvR1HQ8InS25qrhgSsgxhxOYqIKDdhvcfwbdO3x0nVio6MdrqFMSjj60mxyRfV0qsVlTMdyt6jLmOY3yQcpURPalw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Which materials have the highest embodied energy
options.markscheme: A carbon footprint chart reports greenhouse gas emissions (e.g., $\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}$), not embodied energy (typically energy per mass or per unit).

options.content: Which materials have the lowest economic cost
options.markscheme: Carbon footprint data does not provide price/cost information, so cost cannot be identified from this chart alone.
Carbon footprint is an environmental metric, so the chart helps compare the environmental impact (in terms of emissions) of different material choices.

options.content: Which materials have the best mechanical strength
options.markscheme: Mechanical strength is not indicated by carbon footprint data, so it cannot be determined from this chart.

questions.specification: Why might a designer use a simplified life-cycle analysis?

options.content: To assess aesthetic value of a product
To quickly identify the most impactful stages
To generate an exact carbon footprint

questions.specification:
Why might this infographic of electronic waste disposal be concerning in a life-cycle analysis?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/668ef381-04f2-40c5-8e3a-f094fdfcee61.jpeg)

options.content: It supports innovation in digital marketing
options.markscheme: Digital marketing innovation is not an environmental concern or a relevant factor in the waste disposal stage of a life-cycle analysis.

options.content: It reduces dependence on rare earth metals
options.markscheme: Reducing dependence on rare earth metals (through recycling) would be an environmental benefit, whereas the infographic shows low recycling rates, suggesting the opposite.

options.content: It reflects effective use of biodegradable packaging
options.markscheme: Biodegradable packaging is not typically associated with electronic waste disposal statistics and would be considered an environmental advantage rather than a concern.

The infographic demonstrates rapidly increasing e-waste volumes and very low recycling rates. This is concerning in a life-cycle analysis because it indicates significant long-term environmental impacts, such as resource depletion and toxic contamination of soil and water.

questions.specification:
Why is this composting symbol relevant to Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc6nZVuEhEzch6ZhefHod76NA-oQvI2uU7xFc4MZo8Yp8MgOrbJ0oDxSsvamUc5958AhmSps6R3pJZCcCOEqJq65Zeh7pf9VpeDBX1QECVyioRBwvmFAmeNdvg5G79rpzwLsL9Xew?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: The composting symbol identifies a product as compostable, which informs the 'disposal' stage of a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) by signaling a biodegradable end-of-life path.

questions.specification:
Which part of the life-cycle shown in the image contributes most to carbon emissions?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXexYClf7AR7prECQT4pJRD0iV39xlQixlqSsO0Pv_c_rLQXR949rtF2e2qL-35ArJElHSm_ZdXfgj_RHdP2EF-v031s9NU9-cgZ6a3S6eTKbVrCdR31lOSP6F25RLELnVw9wnPkGA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

What environmental concern is likely associated with this image of intensive mining?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/3a56e4a2-8b58-443f-aca5-231512515d04.jpeg)
What aspect of LCA can influence design decisions early in the process?

parts.content: Define life-cycle analysis (LCA).
parts.markscheme:
- Define LCA as a technique used to evaluate the environmental impacts of a product across its entire life cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify that it includes all stages from raw material extraction to final disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each relevant definition component, up to [2 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following would most likely be evaluated in the end-of-life phase?
Which factor is most relevant in the distribution phase of a life-cycle analysis?
A company that designs home lighting products is reviewing the life-cycle impacts of its latest desk lamp.

parts.content:
State the life-cycle stage represented in **Figure 1** below
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/434d2551-4766-45d4-b7a6-6e1a12636ec6)
parts.markscheme: - Raw material acquisition <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two environmental concerns related to aluminum production.
parts.markscheme:
- High energy use during extraction and refining <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Emissions from mining operations affecting local ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows an LED desk lamp in use on a student’s desk.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/rwB2Q1rGxK126wGAO3I--)

Describe three ways this product's use phase can be optimized for sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
- Using energy-efficient LED bulbs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designing with motion sensors or timers to reduce unnecessary use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensuring durability to extend the product's lifespan <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the choice of material in the use phase affects a product’s overall life-cycle assessment.
parts.markscheme:
- Materials with low thermal resistance may affect energy efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lightweight materials reduce shipping emissions during distribution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Toxic materials may pose health risks or require special disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Materials requiring frequent maintenance increase environmental load <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows an e-waste recycling center where people can discard their old lamps at.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Wcfd-UfVos7ZynVp_dJuc)

Identify two challenges in recycling electronic lighting products.
parts.markscheme:
- Difficulty separating electronic components from housing materials <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lack of consumer awareness or access to proper disposal channels <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate strategies a designer can use to reduce the environmental impact of desk lamps across their entire life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Select recyclable or biodegradable materials to improve end-of-life outcomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Design for disassembly to simplify repair and recycling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use modular components to allow upgrades and reduce replacement frequency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Minimize packaging and use renewable sources during production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Consider local sourcing to reduce transportation emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include sustainability labels or guides to educate users <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following best defines "embodied energy"?

options.content: The energy required to ship to retailer
The total energy consumed during material extraction, processing and manufacturing
The energy used during consumer use
The energy used in packaging design

questions.specification:
What aspect of sustainability is being evaluated in this energy usage chart of a product's life?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1b1eaf82-2775-4a85-81e0-c075424673b4)

options.content: Carbon footprint across the product life cycle
Material toxicity/eco-toxicity impact across the product life cycle
Water footprint (water consumption) across the product life cycle

questions.specification: What role does consumer behaviour play in a product's life cycle?

options.content: Influences energy use during the use phase

parts.content: Outline four types of pollution evaluated in a life-cycle analysis.
parts.markscheme:
- Air pollution caused by emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water pollution from industrial discharge <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil contamination due to improper waste disposal <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Noise or thermal pollution during manufacturing <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each pollution type with context, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification: The term 'cradle to cradle' implies that:

options.content: the product is incinerated at the end of its life.
options.markscheme: Incineration is characteristic of a 'cradle to grave' model, where the product's materials are not recovered for future use.

options.content: the product is designed for reuse and regeneration.
options.markscheme: 'Cradle to cradle' is a design philosophy that models human industry on nature's processes, where materials are viewed as nutrients circulating in closed-loop cycles.

options.content: the product is designed so its materials can be recycled into new products at the end of its life.
options.markscheme: This describes end-of-life recycling, but 'cradle to cradle' is broader: it intentionally designs closed-loop cycles (technical and biological nutrients) to eliminate the concept of waste through ongoing reuse/regeneration.

options.content: the product is only analyzed during the manufacturing stage.
options.markscheme: A life cycle analysis limited to the manufacturing stage (from extraction to the factory gate) is known as 'cradle to gate'.

parts.content: Evaluate how transportation affects the overall sustainability of a product.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that long-distance shipping increases carbon emissions and the overall carbon footprint of a product. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Compare** different freight modes, noting that they produce varying environmental footprints (e.g., air versus sea freight). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** how fuel consumption during transportation contributes to the depletion of non-renewable resources. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** how sustainable packaging can reduce the volume and weight of a product, leading to more efficient transportation and reduced fuel use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Analyze** how local sourcing and production can significantly reduce transportation needs and environmental impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Evaluate** the use of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to assess the trade-offs between various transport modes throughout a product's life. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-developed point, up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What information would **NOT** be typically included in a full LCA?

parts.content: Identify two environmental impacts considered in a life-cycle analysis.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** a valid environmental impact, such as global warming potential <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** a second valid environmental impact, such as water pollution <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each valid environmental impact named, up to [2 marks max]. Other examples include ozone depletion, acidification, or resource depletion.</note>

parts.content: Describe the "cradle-to-grave" concept in life-cycle analysis.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the initial stage of raw material extraction (the cradle) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** the inclusion of manufacturing and processing stages <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outline** the distribution, use, and maintenance stages <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** the final stage of disposal, recycling, or waste treatment (the grave) <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each correct stage of the process, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification:
Which part of the product’s life cycle is represented by this shipping container map?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/ccbcd4d1-e7b9-40c6-aaec-3edd5a0e557f.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Correct. Shipping containers and international shipping routes represent the distribution and transport phase of a product's life cycle, where goods are moved from manufacturing sites to global markets.
Incorrect. The use and maintenance phase involves the consumer's interaction with the product and its upkeep, not its logistics.
Incorrect. Retail display relates to how a product is presented to customers in a store, which is a localized marketing activity rather than global distribution.
Incorrect. End-of-life disposal refers to recycling, landfilling, or incinerating a product once it is no longer functional or needed.

questions.specification: How does life-cycle thinking support sustainability goals?

options.markscheme: Correct: life-cycle thinking considers impacts from raw material extraction through manufacture, use, and end-of-life, helping reduce total environmental impact.

options.content: It focuses only on impacts during the manufacturing stage
options.markscheme: Incorrect: life-cycle thinking evaluates environmental impacts across the whole life cycle, not just manufacturing.

options.content: It prioritizes lowest cost over environmental considerations
options.markscheme: Incorrect: life-cycle thinking is used to identify and reduce environmental burdens, not to prioritise cost alone.

options.content: It considers only the product’s appearance rather than its impacts
options.markscheme: Incorrect: life-cycle thinking assesses environmental impacts, not just aesthetics.

questions.specification: What is the main purpose of a life-cycle analysis in product design?

options.content: To select materials with the highest tensile strength
To evaluate environmental impacts of a product from cradle to grave

questions.specification: A sustainability audit is being conducted at a large packaging company. Designers are comparing the life-cycle impacts of common everyday products to improve future designs and reduce emissions across production, use, and disposal.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a row of identical plastic bottles being produced on a factory assembly line.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8py8P6zlCqUY7BCRIviLN)

Identify the life-cycle stage shown in the image.
parts.markscheme: - Manufacture <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two environmental impacts of this stage.
parts.markscheme:
- High energy consumption from injection molding machinery <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potential release of VOCs (volatile organic compounds) during production <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows recycling bins containing paper, metal cans, and plastic containers.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/el_cYBeWfORrPyJQeSbi0)

Explain one benefit of separating materials at the disposal stage of a product's life cycle.
parts.markscheme: - Improves material recovery rates and reduces contamination during recycling <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the disposal stage of products made from recyclable vs non-recyclable materials.
parts.markscheme:
- Recyclable materials like aluminium can be reprocessed and reused multiple times <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Non-recyclables such as composite laminates often end up in landfills <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recycling reduces the demand for virgin raw materials <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Landfill or incineration increases emissions and contributes to long-term pollution <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a delivery truck transporting multiple cardboard boxes across a highway.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/lVRtq_tyzO5aAb65SY_N7)

Describe three ways transportation affects the environmental performance of a product.
parts.markscheme:
- Fuel consumption contributes to CO₂ emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Long-distance shipping increases embodied energy of products <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inefficient packaging increases the number of trips required <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the sustainability of the distribution stage across three different transportation methods: truck, rail, and ship.
parts.markscheme:
- Trucks are flexible but emit higher CO₂ per unit due to fossil fuel usage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rail has lower emissions and is efficient for long distances, but less flexible <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shipping is most efficient per ton-km but contributes to oceanic pollution and port delays <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Choice depends on distance, speed, infrastructure, and carbon footprint trade-offs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Optimizing logistics (e.g., route planning, load efficiency) improves sustainability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers must balance speed, cost, and environmental impact in transportation decisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What stage of the product life cycle considers energy consumption by users?

options.markscheme: The 'use' or 'utilization' stage is when the product is in operation by the consumer, which is when energy consumption typically occurs for electrical or mechanical products.
Pre-production involves the extraction and processing of raw materials.
Packaging is part of the distribution stage, focusing on protection and transportation of the product.
Disposal refers to the end-of-life stage, including recycling, reuse, or landfill.

questions.specification: A sustainable design firm is assessing the environmental impact of three everyday products using life-cycle analysis (LCA). They aim to improve sustainability across all stages of each product’s life span.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a cardboard coffee takeaway cup with a plastic lid, discarded on the ground outside a busy café.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/vKfCFeoTS-0asXcIL93De)

Discuss three ways the use of a cardboard cup with a plastic lid impacts the product’s life-cycle sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
- Cardboard component is typically from renewable resources (e.g. responsibly managed forests) but still has impacts from forestry and pulping <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Plastic lid is usually fossil-fuel derived and increases non-renewable resource use and embodied carbon in raw material stage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Composite construction (card + plastic, and often plastic lining) makes separation difficult, reducing recyclability and increasing likelihood of landfill/incineration at end of life <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Disposable, single-use nature increases waste generation and increases demand for continuous manufacture compared with reusable alternatives <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Littering can cause local environmental harm (visual pollution, wildlife impacts) and increases clean-up requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for distinct sustainability impacts tied to life-cycle stages (materials, manufacture, distribution, use, disposal).*

parts.content: Evaluate how the cardboard coffee takeaway cup with a plastic lid performs across the five stages of life-cycle analysis (LCA).
parts.markscheme:
- Raw material extraction: cardboard may be renewable but forestry/pulp production uses land, water and chemicals; plastic lid relies on petrochemicals <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Manufacturing: forming the cup, adding any barrier lining, and moulding the lid require energy and can generate emissions/waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transport/distribution: light weight reduces transport emissions per unit, but high volumes for single-use items increase total transport impacts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use: convenient but designed for single use, so the use stage does not offset production impacts through repeated use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disposal: contamination and mixed materials can prevent recycling; likely landfill/incineration increases environmental burden; littering worsens impacts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall: although lightweight, the single-use design and end-of-life limitations generally make it less sustainable than reusable alternatives <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a balanced evaluation covering all five LCA stages with environmental insight.*

parts.content:
Figure 2 shows an electric bicycle being used by a commuter in an urban environment.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9b11882c-8587-4471-b82b-05d599c3b69a)

Explain one way the use stage of the electric bicycle contributes positively to sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduces reliance on fossil fuel transportation, lowering carbon emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for a valid and clear link between use stage and environmental benefit.*

parts.content: Describe two sources of environmental impact during the distribution and transport stage of the electric bicycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Fuel consumption from overseas shipping of bike components <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Packaging waste generated during delivery to retailers <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for clearly explained environmental effects of distribution and logistics.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a simplified diagram of the life-cycle stages of a stainless steel water bottle, including raw material extraction, manufacture, transport, use, and disposal.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/da925448-4908-4d64-83d8-bc159e879668)

Discuss three ways the use of stainless steel impacts the product’s life-cycle sustainability compared to plastic.
parts.markscheme:
- More energy-intensive to produce, increasing emissions during manufacture <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Durable and long-lasting, reducing need for frequent replacement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fully recyclable at end of life, supporting closed-loop systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for distinct sustainability comparisons tied to life-cycle stages.*

parts.content: Evaluate how the stainless steel water bottle performs across the five stages of life-cycle analysis (LCA).
parts.markscheme:
- Raw material extraction has a high environmental cost due to mining <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Manufacturing requires high heat and energy, increasing emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Efficient distribution can minimize carbon footprint depending on transport method <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reusable design extends product life, significantly reducing waste over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Final disposal is efficient since stainless steel is recyclable in many regions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, despite high upfront impact, its reusability and recyclability make it a sustainable long-term choice <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a balanced evaluation covering all five LCA stages with environmental insight.*

questions.specification: What is a limitation of simplified LCAs compared to full LCAs?

options.content: They may omit critical environmental details
They focus only on product aesthetics

questions.specification: What does the 'grave' represent in cradle-to-grave analysis?

options.content: The point at which patents expire
The disposal or recycling of the product

questions.specification: What tool is most commonly used to quantify life-cycle environmental impact?
